title: Library Closing Announcement
transcript: Attention, please. The university library will close at six o'clock this Friday because of scheduled electrical maintenance. Students who need to borrow books should do so before five thirty. The online library system will remain available during the evening.
selection_reason: Part 1 announcement trains listening for time, reason and instruction.

title: Train Platform Change
description: Short transport announcement for Listening Part 1.
transcript: Passengers for the 9:15 train to Greenfield should go to platform seven. This is a platform change. The train will not depart from platform three as shown on earlier tickets. Staff members are available near the information desk if you need help.
selection_reason: Transport announcements are realistic short-listening items with one key detail.

title: Outdoor Event Weather Notice
description: Short weather-related notice for Listening Part 1.
transcript: Because of heavy rain expected this afternoon, the school music performance will move from the playground to the main hall. The starting time is still two o'clock. Parents should enter through Gate B because Gate A is being repaired.
selection_reason: Part 1 location-change announcements train gist plus detail recognition.

title: Clinic Appointment Reminder
description: Short appointment reminder for Listening Part 1.
transcript: This is a reminder from City Clinic. Your health check appointment is tomorrow at 10:30 a.m. Please arrive ten minutes early and bring your ID card. If you need to change the appointment, call us before five o'clock today.
selection_reason: Appointment reminders are common instruction texts for detail listening.

title: Roommates Discuss a Study Plan
transcript: Maya: I still have three chapters to review before Friday's test. I don't think reading everything again will help. Tom: Why don't we make a question list instead? If we test each other, we may remember more. Maya: Good idea. But I also need to practice the vocabulary section. Tom: Let's spend thirty minutes on vocabulary first, then forty minutes on the difficult chapter. Maya: Can we meet in the library after lunch? My room is too noisy. Tom: Sure. I'll book a group-study table for one thirty.
selection_reason: Part 2 conversation trains listening for agreement, sequence and reason.

title: Changing a Job Interview Time
transcript: Recruiter: Good morning, Ms. Linh. I'm calling about your interview for the marketing assistant position. Candidate: Good morning. Is there a problem with my application? Recruiter: Not at all. The manager has an urgent client meeting at ten, so we need to move your interview from ten thirty to two o'clock. Candidate: That works for me. Should I still bring my portfolio? Recruiter: Yes, please. Also prepare a short explanation of one campaign you worked on. Candidate: Thank you. I'll arrive fifteen minutes early. Recruiter: Excellent. Reception will give you a visitor card when you arrive.
selection_reason: Part 2 workplace conversation supports purpose, detail and inference questions.

title: Talk on Urban Farming
transcript: Today I want to talk about urban farming, a practice that is becoming more popular in many large cities. Urban farms can be built on rooftops, unused land or even inside buildings with artificial light. Supporters say these farms make cities greener and help residents understand where food comes from. They can also reduce transport distance for some vegetables. However, urban farming is not a complete solution to food security. Land in cities is expensive, and indoor systems may use a lot of energy. The most successful projects usually combine food production with education, community activities and careful environmental planning. In other words, urban farms work best when they are designed as social spaces as well as places to grow food.
selection_reason: Part 3 talk provides extended explanation with advantages, limitations and conclusion.

title: Lecture on Digital Privacy
transcript: Digital privacy is often discussed as an individual choice: users are told to read privacy policies, change settings and avoid suspicious websites. These actions are useful, but they are not enough. Modern data collection is complex and often invisible. A person may agree to share location data with one application, but that information can later be combined with shopping records or advertising profiles. This creates a detailed picture of behavior that users rarely see. For this reason, many experts argue that privacy protection requires stronger regulation, clearer company responsibility and better public education. The goal is not to stop all data use. Data can improve services and support research. The challenge is to ensure that collection is limited, transparent and genuinely necessary.
selection_reason: Part 3 lecture supports C1 inference and attitude questions about abstract technology policy.
